does: This paper demonstrates that using GPT-4 to generate synthetic instruction-following training data significantly improves zero-shot task performance in finetuned LLaMA models. The authors create 52K English and Chinese instruction examples through GPT-4, then finetune LLaMA on this data and evaluate the resulting model across diverse unseen tasks. They additionally leverage GPT-4 to generate comparative feedback and evaluation data, enabling both comprehensive assessment of model quality and training of reward models. The key contribution is showing that higher-quality synthetic data from a more capable model (GPT-4) outperforms previous approaches like self-instruct data generation, establishing a new standard for instruction data quality despite not requiring any human annotation.
connects: This work builds directly on the instruction-tuning paradigm established by InstructGPT and fine-tuning approaches that showed LLMs could gain strong zero-shot generalization through instruction-following data. It extends earlier work on self-instruct and machine-generated training data, which demonstrated that models could bootstrap their own training examples without human annotation. The paper responds to the limitation that prior synthetic data generation relied on weaker models, proposing instead to harness GPT-4's superior capabilities as a data generator. It also connects to the growing interest in using LLM-generated feedback for evaluation and reward model training, scaling evaluation beyond traditional benchmarks.
enables: This work opens the door to using frontier models as high-quality synthetic data generators, a paradigm that could accelerate development of capable smaller models without large-scale human annotation. It suggests future approaches could iteratively improve models by leveraging the best available model as a data source for training improved variants. The public release of GPT-4-generated data and codebase enables the community to explore data quality effects systematically and build upon this foundation. It also points toward using LLM-generated feedback at scale for evaluation and reward modeling, potentially automating much of the pipeline for training aligned, task-generalized models and reducing dependence on expensive human labeling.
